text: The colloanal sleeve operation is a workhorse to get out of trouble in the pelvis. If you have a stapler misfire low in the pelvis, then converting this to an endoanal colloanal anastomosis can get you out of trouble.

When there is a patient who has a previously failed anastomosis with a pelvic abscess, pelvis is hostile, and sometimes the safest place to be is within the intestinal lumen. The pull-through operation can be used in those circumstances.
It can be used with a fistula to the vagina or perineum, or in the circumstances of a rectourethral fistula.
The surgeon mobilizes the left colon thoroughly and dissects the rectum down to the point of maximum difficulty before dividing it at that level.
The surgeon performs a mucozectomy from above and below to the dentate line. The splenic flexure is mobilized, with the colon running along the marginal artery.
The surgeon passes the colon through the mucosectomized rectum to the anal canal, where it is hand-anastomosed. A loop ileostomy is constructed at the end of the procedure.

The patient is a seventy-eight-year-old man who in the past had had a radical prostatectomy for prostate cancer. Subsequently, he developed a rectal cancer for which he received chemoradiation and then a low anterior resection. Regrettably, he then developed a fistula from the anastomosis to the previous radical prostatectomy site as a rectourethral fistula.
The planned operation is Suave's colloanal pull-through anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a full-length laparotomy, mobilizing the splenic flexure. The colon is visually observed running along the marginal artery, with ample reach in the left colon.
The operation has been performed deep into the pelvis to the point of difficulty.
The surgeon uses a budget-friendly version of the Lone Star retractor to maintain exposure in the surgical field.
The surgeon demonstrates the fistula connecting the rectal anastomosis site to the prostatic cavity, highlighting the abnormal passage between these anatomical structures.

The surgeon raises a mucosal bleb using dilute adrenaline and disconnects the dentate line with scissors. The mucozectomy begins, carefully preserving the underlying internal sphincter, and extends up to the divided rectal segment.
The surgeon removes strips of mucosa from the anal region, ensuring careful dissection to preserve underlying structures.
It can be appreciated how much easier the operation is if the distal mucosectomy is as short as possible.
The surgeon curettes the fistula track and cavity, removing tissue debris from the area.
The surgeon delivers the colon through the anus, confirms its orientation, and opens the lumen, ensuring there is no twist in the mesentery.

The surgeon begins the anastomosis with the colon hanging outside the anal canal, placing the first four sutures. The prolapsing colon is then reduced, and the sutures are tied, achieving a tension-free anastomosis.

The Eisenhammer retractor is positioned within the anal canal, allowing visualization of each hour of the clock face. The surgeon reverses and rotates the suture needle to facilitate transanal suturing, while an assistant assists by grabbing the needle tip and passing it kephalad before withdrawing it from the anus. The anal dilatation is minimized, occurring only during brief moments when the retractor is in place.
This is a marked difference to the prolonged anal dilatation time seen with the standard Lone Star retractor.
The surgeon checks the anastomosis for integrity and inserts any additional sutures as needed. The stay sutures are removed, allowing the anus to return to its normal configuration.

The following tips are helpful. Perform as much of the rectal dissection abdominally as possible. This is because abdominal mucozectomy is difficult. The safest point in a hazardous pelvis is inside the bowel lumen. Be prepared to split the rectal tube in the midline posteriorly and open it like a book. Hang the colon outside the anus before reducing to ensure lack of tension. Imagine an open and then a closed accordion. If possible, bring down an adjunctive omentoplasty.
The suave coloanal sleeve anastomosis is an essential part of a colorectal surgeon's armamentarium.
